title: Annual Report on Open Market Operations

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: SOMA holdings of Treasuries and agency MBS decreased by nearly $700 billion during 2024 as balance sheet runoff continued under FOMC-directed monthly caps. The FOMC reduced the Treasury securities redemption cap from $60 billion to $25 billion in June 2024 while maintaining the agency MBS cap at $35 billion.
key_points: SOMA holdings of Treasuries and agency MBS decreased by nearly $700 billion during 2024
SOMA securities comprise approximately 95 percent of Federal Reserve assets
FOMC reduced Treasury securities redemption cap from $60 billion to $25 billion in June 2024
Treasury securities holdings declined from $4.79 trillion to $4.29 trillion, representing about 70 percent of total SOMA runoff
Agency MBS cap remained unchanged at $35 billion; holdings decreased from $2.42 trillion to $2.23 trillion
Agency MBS represented approximately 30 percent of total SOMA runoff
why_it_matters: This document provides official Federal Reserve data on balance sheet normalization, a critical aspect of monetary policy implementation that affects market liquidity, Treasury supply dynamics, and the broader financial conditions framework.
macro_context: Balance sheet runoff represents the Federal Reserve's passive monetary tightening mechanism, removing duration and liquidity from the financial system as the central bank allows securities to mature without reinvestment.
market_structure_context: The FOMC's management of SOMA runoff caps directly controls the pace at which Federal Reserve assets leave the balance sheet, influencing Treasury and agency MBS market supply dynamics.

notes: Source identified as Federal Reserve Bank of New York based on SOMA context and domain trust tier. The data represents official Federal Reserve balance sheet operations data for 2024. Numbers are presented in billions or trillions as appropriate for context.
